questions.prompt: A team creates a new Stripe Product every time they tweak pricing. They end up with hundreds of Products and messy reporting. Right structure?

choices.text: One Product per plan (Pro, Enterprise, etc.), multiple Prices per Product (monthly, yearly, currency variants, tier variants). Products are the abstract offering; Prices are the specific pricing. Subscriptions reference Prices. This shape lets you change pricing without losing product identity in reporting.

questions.explanation: Products = abstract offerings. Prices = specific pricing for those offerings. One Product can host many Prices (monthly/yearly, currency variants, tiers). This keeps reporting clean and lets pricing evolve without polluting the catalog.

questions.prompt: What's the right pattern for handling 3DS on subscription signup?

choices.text: Create subscription with `payment_behavior: 'default_incomplete'` and `expand: ['latest_invoice.payment_intent']`. Send the resulting `client_secret` to frontend; Stripe.js `confirmPayment` handles 3DS. Subscription stays `incomplete` until first payment succeeds, then becomes `active`. Clean pattern for handling auth challenges at signup.
Charge first; ask for auth later.

questions.explanation: `default_incomplete` is the canonical pattern for subscription creation that handles 3DS gracefully. The frontend confirms the first payment via Stripe.js (which handles challenges); subscription becomes active after success. Without this, 3DS on signup leaves subscriptions stuck in `incomplete` forever.
